元[[准勅祭社]] |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = [[権現造]] |別名 = 根津権現{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |札所等 = [[東京十社]] |例祭 = [[9月21日]]{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |神事 = }} [[ファイル:Nedu-Jinja Omotesando.jpg|thumb|200px|right|表参道鳥居
社号票の揮毫は[[平田東助]]内務大臣]] '''根津神社'''（ねづじんじゃ）は、[[東京都]][[文京区]][[根津]]にある[[神社]]。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[府社]]で、元[[東京十社|准勅祭社（東京十社）]]。[[つつじ]]の名所として有名。 == 概要 == [[ヤマトタケル|日本武尊]]が1900年近く前に創祀したと伝える古社で、[[東京十社]]の一社に数えられている。 現在の社殿は[[宝永]]3年（[[1706年]]）、[[甲府藩]]主の[[徳川家宣|徳川綱豊]]（後の[[江戸幕府]]第6代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]]徳川家宣）が献納した屋敷地に造営されたものである。[[権現造]]（本殿、幣殿、拝殿を構造的に一体に造る）の傑作とされている。社殿7棟が国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている[https://nedujinja.or.jp/keidaiannai/ 境内案内] - 根津神社。 境内は[[ツツジ]]の名所として知られる。また根津神社の近くには、[[森鷗外]]が文京区に移住してから最初に住み、後に[[夏目漱石]]が住んだこともある千朶山房（せんださんぼう）や、鷗外が後半生に暮らした観潮楼（かんちょうろう）が近かったこともあり、これらの文豪に因んだ旧跡も残されている。特に鷗外は根津神社の氏子で、小説『青年』に「根津権現」として登場するなど縁があり、2021年に閉館した旅館「水月ホテル鷗外荘」（台東区池之端）で使用されていた鷗外の旧邸も根津神社に移築されることになった。 == 社名 == 根津神社は、江戸時代には[[山王神道]]の権現社であり、当時は「'''[[#歴史|根津権現]]'''」とも称された。この呼称は明治初期の[[神仏分離]]の際に「[[権現]]」の称が一時期禁止されたために衰退したが、地元では現在も使われる場合がある。単に「権現様」とも称される{{efn|ただし、現在では山王権現は祭神とはされず、大山咋命が祀られている。}}。文学作品では「根津権現」として出てくることが多い。 == 祭神 == ; 主祭神 * [[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]（すさのおのみこと） * [[大山咋神]]（おおやまくいのかみ） * [[応神天皇|誉田別命]]（ほんだわけのみこと） [[神仏習合]]下での垂迹神と本地は以下の通り。 {| class="wikitable" ! !!垂迹神（旧称）!!本地 |- |rowspan=3|根津三所権現 |素盞烏尊||[[十一面観音|十一面観音菩薩]] |- |[[山王権現|山王大権現]]||[[薬師如来]] |- |[[八幡神|八幡大菩薩]]||[[阿弥陀如来]] |} ; 相殿 * [[大国主|大国主神]] （おおくにぬしのかみ） * [[菅原道真|菅原道真公]] （すがわらのみちざねこう） == 歴史 == 『根津志』によれば、「抑根津大権現往古勧請の年歴を知らず。駒込惣鎮守ニて千駄木村に鎮座し給ふ。神躰は素盞烏尊本地十一面観世音菩薩、 相殿二社山王大権現本地薬師如来、八幡宮本地阿弥陀如来、是を根津三所大権現と申奉る。中頃太田道灌入道持資の再興ともいふ。」と記され、1900年ほど前に[[日本武尊]]が[[千駄木]]に創祀したとされ、[[文明 (日本)|文明]]年間（[[1469年]]-[[1486年]]）には[[太田道灌]]により社殿が造られたと伝わる。 [[江戸時代]]になると、[[天台宗]]の医王山正運寺昌泉院が[[神宮寺]]（別当）を務め文京区神社誌、根津大権現の社は[[山王神道]]の権現社となった。主祭神の[[スサノオ|素盞烏尊]]は十一面観音菩薩を[[本地仏]]として祀られ、[[相殿]]に祀られた山王大権現（本地仏は薬師如来）や[[八幡神]]（本地仏は阿弥陀如来）と合わせて'''根津三所権現'''とも呼ばれた。 [[万治]]年間（[[1658年]]-[[1661年]]）に同所が[[太田氏]]の屋敷地となったため東方に移り、のちさらに団子坂上（現文京区立本郷図書館周辺、元根津）に遷座した『東京都の地名』根津神社項。。また、『[[江戸名所記]]』（[[寛文]]2年/[[1662年]]刊）には、「根津権現は不寝（ねず）権現であり、諸神の寝ずの番衆であろう」と記されている。 根津神社が現在の姿を整えたのは、江戸幕府第5代将軍の[[徳川綱吉]]の治下にあった宝永3年（1706年）である。当時嗣子のなかった綱吉は、甥で甲府藩主の綱豊（綱吉の兄・[[徳川綱重|綱重]]の子）を世嗣に定めたが、根津にあった[[甲府徳川家]]の江戸屋敷で出生した綱豊は根津権現を[[産土神]]としていたことから、綱豊が[[江戸城]]へ移る際に藩邸跡を根津権現へ献納し、社殿を造営したのである[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - 根津神社。工事には諸大名が動員され、社殿造営は[[天下普請]]と言われた。そのため、綱豊から改名した家宣とその実子の[[徳川家継|家継]]が第6代・第7代将軍としてそれぞれ在職した時期、根津権現は[[徳川宗家|徳川将軍家]]の崇敬を集めた。特に、[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]4年（[[1714年]]）[[9月21日]]に実施された[[例祭]]では、[[江戸城]]内に[[神輿]]が入ることを許されたため、[[天下祭]]の一つに挙げられた。しかし、家継没後に[[紀州徳川家]]出身の[[徳川吉宗]]が第8代将軍に就任すると、[[享保の改革]]の一環で、例祭は公営から民営に切り換えられ、地味なものになった。 その後の[[明治維新]]では、神仏分離・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、山王神道に基づく根津権現の祭祀は廃され、当時の[[国家神道]]に基づき、本殿にはスサノオ、大山咋神、八幡神を、相殿には大国主と菅原道真を祀る形式へ強制的に改組された。 また、[[東京大学|帝国大学]]の移転にともない、江戸時代から門前に形成されていた[[根津遊廓]]は[[明治]]21年（[[1888年]]）6月末に廃され、江東区の[[洲崎 (東京都)|洲崎遊廓]]へと移転した。 == 境内 == === 社殿 === 社殿は江戸幕府第5代将軍・徳川綱吉による造営で、本殿・幣殿・拝殿が1つにまとめられた[[権現造]]の形式である。 ファイル:根津神社 本殿.JPG|本殿（重要文化財） ファイル:根津神社 拝殿.JPG|拝殿（重要文化財） ファイル:根津神社 唐門.JPG|唐門と透塀（ともに重要文化財） File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 3.jpg|透塀（重要文化財） ファイル:根津神社 神楽殿.JPG|神楽殿 ファイル:Nezu Shrine Romon 2010.jpg|楼門（重要文化財） ファイル:Komainu of Nezu Shrine left.jpg|狛犬 === 摂末社 === * 乙女稲荷神社 - 祭神：[[倉稲魂命]] : 根津神社の遷座時、境内西側の傾斜面（つつじが岡）の中腹に洞を穿つ形で祀られた。 * 駒込稲荷神社 - 祭神：[[伊弉諾命]]、[[伊弉冊命]]、[[倉稲魂命]]、[[級長津彦命]]、[[級長戸辺命]] : 寛文元年（1661年）鎮座。旧甲府藩邸時代の守り神として祀られたもので、本殿の遷座に伴って末社となった。[[浜離宮]]内の稲荷神社の分祀元。 ファイル:Otome Inari Shrine 2010.jpg|乙女稲荷神社 ファイル:駒込稲荷神社 (根津神社境内社).JPG|駒込稲荷神社 === その他 === * [[徳川家宣]][[胎盤|胞衣]]塚 - 当社遷座前、甲府藩邸時代に甲府徳川家嫡子・綱豊（のちに甲府藩主を経て6代将軍家宣となる）の胞衣（胎児を包んだ膜と[[胎盤]]）を埋めた時のもの。 * [[岐の神|塞大神]]碑 - 本郷追分（東大農学部前の[[中山道]]・[[日光御成街道]]分岐路）に祀られていた[[道祖神]]が立っている。 * 家宣の産湯井戸（非公開） * 庚申塔 - 六基。近隣の町の辻にあったものが道路拡幅などで撤去されたのを引き取ったもの。 * つつじ園 - 旧藩邸時代に家宣が植えたのが最初。現在も栽培されており、開花期（春-初夏）には一般開放されている。 * [[森鷗外]]旧邸 - 2021年（令和3年）に閉店した旅館「水月ホテル鷗外荘」（台東区池之端）にあった建物で、閉店後に移築を引き受けることになり、根津神社の総代会の全会一致で移築が決定した{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/207353|title=森鷗外が「舞姫」を執筆した旧邸、ゆかりの根津神社に移築へ 旅館として稼働もコロナ禍で閉館|date=2022-10-10|publisher=東京新聞|accessdate=2022-10-12}}。この建物は鷗外が最初の妻の赤松登志子と1889年5月から住んでいた邸宅で、1890年11月の離婚の直前の文京区への転居まで居住していた。1946年（昭和21年）に旅館の創業者が隣接する旧邸を買い取った。旅館「水月ホテル鷗外荘」での解体工事は2022年10月3日から始まり、根津神社境内東側への移設が検討されている。 [[文京つつじまつり|つつじまつり]] * 6月 ** 大祓 * 9月 ** 例大祭神賑行事（[[9月17日]]-[[9月18日|18日]]） ** 例祭式（[[9月21日]]） * 10月 ** 根津・千駄木下町まつり（10月20日頃） * 11月 ** 七五三詣 ** 新嘗祭（[[11月23日]]） * 12月 ** 大祓（[[12月31日]]） == 文化財 == [[File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 2.jpg|thumb|200px|透塀（西門北方]] [[File:Nezu-jinja Außenbereich 2.jpg|thumb|200px|西門]] === 重要文化財 === ; 建造物 * 根津神社 7棟 ** 本殿・幣殿・拝殿（1棟） ** 唐門 ** 西門 ** 透塀（3棟）（唐門東方、唐門西門間、西門北方） ** 楼門 ** （附指定）銅灯籠2基 ; 工芸品 * 太刀 銘長光 * 太刀 銘備州長船秀光　康暦二年二月 日 === 文京区指定文化財 === * 神楽面 16面（彫刻） - 平成6年指定 * 神輿 3基（附 獅子2頭）（工芸） - 昭和55年指定 : 正徳4年（1714年）、遷宮記念の大祭（天下祭、宝永祭）において奉納されたもの。神輿の巡行に際して、獅子が行列を先導した。 * 徳川氏朱印状 8通（古文書） - 昭和49年指定 * 徳川家宣胞衣塚（有形民俗） - 昭和49年指定 == 氏子地域 == : [[文京区]][[根津]] : 文京区[[千駄木]]一～二丁目全域、三丁目37～51 : 文京区[[本駒込]]一丁目2～4･9～25、二丁目1～9･13～17（このうち旧駒込曙町の部分）、三丁目1～9（このうち旧駒込浅嘉町の部分） : 文京区[[向丘 (文京区)|向丘]] : 文京区[[弥生 (文京区)|弥生]] : 文京区[[本郷 (文京区)|本郷]]五丁目30～32（このうち旧本郷森川町の部分）、六丁目全域 : 文京区[[西片]]二丁目23～25 : [[台東区]][[池之端]]一丁目2～6・二丁目全域 == 現地情報 == ; 所在地 * 東京都文京区根津一丁目28番9号 ; 交通アクセス * [[ファイル:Logo of Tokyo Metro Chiyoda Line.svg|18px|C]] [[東京メトロ千代田線]] [[根津駅]]・[[千駄木駅]]（各駅とも徒歩5分） * [[ファイル:Logo of Tokyo Metro Namboku Line.svg|18px|N]] [[東京メトロ南北線]] [[東大前駅]]（徒歩5分） * [[ファイル:Toei Mita line symbol.svg|18px|I]] [[都営地下鉄三田線|都営三田線]] [[白山駅 (東京都)|白山駅]]（徒歩10分） ; 周辺 * [[日本医科大学]] * 団子坂上 - 現在の文京区立本郷図書館や東洋大学のある辺りが当社の旧鎮座地で、「元根津」とも呼ばれる。 == 登場作品 == * [[森鷗外]]『青年』 {{quotation|小泉純一は芝日蔭町の宿屋を出て、東京方眼図を片手に人にうるさく問うて、新橋停留場から上野行の電車に乗った。目まぐろしい須田町の乗換も無事に済んだ。さて本郷三丁目で電車を降りて、追分（おいわけ）から高等学校に附いて右に曲がって、根津権現の表坂上にある袖浦館という下宿屋の前に到着したのは、十月二十何日かの午前八時であった。}} * [[森鷗外]]『細木香以』[http://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000129/files/4459_18093.html　細木香以]青空文庫 {{quotation|団子坂上から南して根津権現の裏門に出る岨道に似た小径がある。これを藪下の道と云う。}} * [[高村光雲]]『幕末維新懐古談』 {{quotation|従来神田明神とか、根津権現とかいったものは、神田神社、根津神社というようになり、三社権現も浅草神社と改称して、神仏何方（どっち）かに方附けなければならないことになったのである。}} * [[岡本綺堂]]『半七捕物帳（柳原堤の女）』 {{quotation|かれは強情にかんがえた末に、同町内の和泉という建具屋の若い職人を誘い出すことにした。職人は茂八といって、ことしの夏は根津神社の境内まで素人相撲をとりに行った男である。かれは喜平の相談をうけて、一も二もなく承知した。}} * [[夏目漱石]]『道草』 {{quotation|その人は根津権現の裏門の坂を上って、彼と反対に北へ向いて歩いて来たものと見えて、健三が行手を何気なく眺めた時、十間位先から既に彼の視線に入ったのである。そうして思わず彼の眼をわきへ外させたのである。}} {{quotation|こうした無事の日が五日続いた後、六日目の朝になって帽子を被らない男は突然また根津権現の坂の蔭から現われて健三を脅やかした。それがこの前とほぼ同じ場所で、時間も殆どこの前と違わなかった。}} * [[尾崎放哉]]『入庵雑記』 {{quotation|句会は大抵根津権現さんの境内に小さい池に沿うて一寸した貸席がありましたので、其処で開きました。}} * [[岡本綺堂]]『深見夫人の死』 - 下記引用以外にも多数の記述あり {{quotation|その住宅は本郷の根津権現に近いところに在って、門を掩（おお）うている桜の大樹が昔ながらに白く咲き乱れているのも嬉しかった。}} {{quotation|わたしはその賑わいを後ろにして池（いけ）の端（はた）から根津の方角へ急いだ。その頃はまだ動坂（どうざか）行きの電車が開通していなかったので、根津の通りも暗い寂しい町であった。}} * [[寺田寅彦]]『柿の種』 {{quotation|根津権現の境内のある 旗亭 で大学生が数人会していた。 夜がふけて、あたりが静かになったころに、どこかでふくろうの鳴くのが聞こえた。 「ふくろうが鳴くね」 と一人が言った。}} * 佐々木味津三『旗本退屈男（第三話）後の旗本退屈男』 {{quotation|しかも、その六本の白刄を、笑止千万にも必死に擬していたものは、ほんの小半時前、根津権現裏のあの浪宅から、いずれともなく逐電した筈の市毛甚之丞以下おろかしき浪人共でしたから、門を堅く閉じ締めていた理由も、うしろに十数本の槍先を擬しているものの待ち伏せていた理由も、彼等六人の急を知らせたためからであったかと知った退屈男は、急にカンラカンラ打ち笑い出すと、門の外に佇んだままでいる京弥に大きく呼びかけました。}} * [[宮本百合子]]『田端の汽車そのほか』 {{quotation|森鴎外が住んでいた家は、団子坂をのぼってすぐのところにあった。坂をのぼり切ると一本はそのまま真直に肴町へ、右は林町へ折れ、左の一本は細くくねって昔太田ケ原と呼ばれた崖沿いに根津権現に出る。}} * [[林不忘]]『丹下左膳（乾雲坤竜の巻）』 {{quotation|江戸は根津権現の裏、俗に曙の里といわれるところに、神変夢想流の町道場を開いている小野塚鉄斎（おのづかてっさい）、いま奥の書院に端坐して、抜き放った一刀の刀身にあかず見入っている。}} * [[三遊亭圓朝]]（鈴木行三校訂・編纂）『敵討札所の霊験』 {{quotation|ちょうど根津権現へ参詣して、惣門内を抜けて参りましたが、只今でも全盛でございますが、昔から彼の廓は度々たびたび潰れましては又再願をして又立ったと申しますが、其の頃贅沢な女郎がございまして}} * [[夢野久作]]『東京人の堕落時代』 {{quotation|その次は井の頭で、これはどちらかと云えば高級なのが多いらしい。但、夜は高級か低級か保証の限りでない。根津権現はその又次という順序である。その他大小の公園、神社、仏閣、活動館、芝居小屋、カフェー、飲食店なぞが、色魔式の活躍場所である事は云う迄もない。}} * [[武田泰淳]]『目まいのする散歩』 {{quotation|肴町から電車通を横断して、左手に大観音（同じ宗派のその寺には、よく父親の命令でお使いに行った）を見て、根津権現の坂道にかかる。下宿屋の多い急な坂道を下りきって、上野山へと裏側から上って行く。そのあたりには、画学生の匂いがあり、やがて、美術学校や、美術館、博物館の建つ、上野山の西洋風のひろがりの中へ入る。}} == ドラマ == {{神社 |名称 = 根津神社 |画像 = [[File:Nezu Shrine 2010.jpg|280px]]
拝殿（重要文化財）
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|35|43|12.7|N|139|45|38.5|E}}}} |所在地 = {{JPN}}
[[東京都]][[文京区]][[根津]]一丁目28番9号{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|43|12.77|N|139|45|38.50|E|region:JP-13|name=根津神社|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = [[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]
[[オオヤマクイ|大山咋神]]
[[八幡神|誉田別命]]
[[大国主命]]
[[菅原道真|菅原道真公]] |社格 = 旧[[府社]]
元[[准勅祭社]] |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = [[権現造]] |別名 = 根津権現{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |札所等 = [[東京十社]] |例祭 = [[9月21日]]{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |神事 = }} [[ファイル:Nedu-Jinja Omotesando.jpg|thumb|200px|right|表参道鳥居
社号票の揮毫は[[平田東助]]内務大臣]] '''根津神社'''（ねづじんじゃ）は、[[東京都]][[文京区]][[根津]]にある[[神社]]。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[府社]]で、元[[東京十社|准勅祭社（東京十社）]]。[[つつじ]]の名所として有名。 == 概要 == [[ヤマトタケル|日本武尊]]が1900年近く前に創祀したと伝える古社で、[[東京十社]]の一社に数えられている。 現在の社殿は[[宝永]]3年（[[1706年]]）、[[甲府藩]]主の[[徳川家宣|徳川綱豊]]（後の[[江戸幕府]]第6代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]]徳川家宣）が献納した屋敷地に造営されたものである。[[権現造]]（本殿、幣殿、拝殿を構造的に一体に造る）の傑作とされている。社殿7棟が国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている[https://nedujinja.or.jp/keidaiannai/ 境内案内] - 根津神社。 境内は[[ツツジ]]の名所として知られる。また根津神社の近くには、[[森鷗外]]が文京区に移住してから最初に住み、後に[[夏目漱石]]が住んだこともある千朶山房（せんださんぼう）や、鷗外が後半生に暮らした観潮楼（かんちょうろう）が近かったこともあり、これらの文豪に因んだ旧跡も残されている。特に鷗外は根津神社の氏子で、小説『青年』に「根津権現」として登場するなど縁があり、2021年に閉館した旅館「水月ホテル鷗外荘」（台東区池之端）で使用されていた鷗外の旧邸も根津神社に移築されることになった。 == 社名 == 根津神社は、江戸時代には[[山王神道]]の権現社であり、当時は「'''[[#歴史|根津権現]]'''」とも称された。この呼称は明治初期の[[神仏分離]]の際に「[[権現]]」の称が一時期禁止されたために衰退したが、地元では現在も使われる場合がある。単に「権現様」とも称される{{efn|ただし、現在では山王権現は祭神とはされず、大山咋命が祀られている。}}。文学作品では「根津権現」として出てくることが多い。 == 祭神 == ; 主祭神 * [[スサノオ|須佐之男命]]（すさのおのみこと） * [[大山咋神]]（おおやまくいのかみ） * [[応神天皇|誉田別命]]（ほんだわけのみこと） [[神仏習合]]下での垂迹神と本地は以下の通り。 {| class="wikitable" ! !!垂迹神（旧称）!!本地 |- |rowspan=3|根津三所権現 |素盞烏尊||[[十一面観音|十一面観音菩薩]] |- |[[山王権現|山王大権現]]||[[薬師如来]] |- |[[八幡神|八幡大菩薩]]||[[阿弥陀如来]] |} ; 相殿 * [[大国主|大国主神]] （おおくにぬしのかみ） * [[菅原道真|菅原道真公]] （すがわらのみちざねこう） == 歴史 == 『根津志』によれば、「抑根津大権現往古勧請の年歴を知らず。駒込惣鎮守ニて千駄木村に鎮座し給ふ。神躰は素盞烏尊本地十一面観世音菩薩、 相殿二社山王大権現本地薬師如来、八幡宮本地阿弥陀如来、是を根津三所大権現と申奉る。中頃太田道灌入道持資の再興ともいふ。」と記され、1900年ほど前に[[日本武尊]]が[[千駄木]]に創祀したとされ、[[文明 (日本)|文明]]年間（[[1469年]]-[[1486年]]）には[[太田道灌]]により社殿が造られたと伝わる。 [[江戸時代]]になると、[[天台宗]]の医王山正運寺昌泉院が[[神宮寺]]（別当）を務め文京区神社誌、根津大権現の社は[[山王神道]]の権現社となった。主祭神の[[スサノオ|素盞烏尊]]は十一面観音菩薩を[[本地仏]]として祀られ、[[相殿]]に祀られた山王大権現（本地仏は薬師如来）や[[八幡神]]（本地仏は阿弥陀如来）と合わせて'''根津三所権現'''とも呼ばれた。 [[万治]]年間（[[1658年]]-[[1661年]]）に同所が[[太田氏]]の屋敷地となったため東方に移り、のちさらに団子坂上（現文京区立本郷図書館周辺、元根津）に遷座した『東京都の地名』根津神社項。。また、『[[江戸名所記]]』（[[寛文]]2年/[[1662年]]刊）には、「根津権現は不寝（ねず）権現であり、諸神の寝ずの番衆であろう」と記されている。 根津神社が現在の姿を整えたのは、江戸幕府第5代将軍の[[徳川綱吉]]の治下にあった宝永3年（1706年）である。当時嗣子のなかった綱吉は、甥で甲府藩主の綱豊（綱吉の兄・[[徳川綱重|綱重]]の子）を世嗣に定めたが、根津にあった[[甲府徳川家]]の江戸屋敷で出生した綱豊は根津権現を[[産土神]]としていたことから、綱豊が[[江戸城]]へ移る際に藩邸跡を根津権現へ献納し、社殿を造営したのである[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - 根津神社。工事には諸大名が動員され、社殿造営は[[天下普請]]と言われた。そのため、綱豊から改名した家宣とその実子の[[徳川家継|家継]]が第6代・第7代将軍としてそれぞれ在職した時期、根津権現は[[徳川宗家|徳川将軍家]]の崇敬を集めた。特に、[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]4年（[[1714年]]）[[9月21日]]に実施された[[例祭]]では、[[江戸城]]内に[[神輿]]が入ることを許されたため、[[天下祭]]の一つに挙げられた。しかし、家継没後に[[紀州徳川家]]出身の[[徳川吉宗]]が第8代将軍に就任すると、[[享保の改革]]の一環で、例祭は公営から民営に切り換えられ、地味なものになった。 その後の[[明治維新]]では、神仏分離・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、山王神道に基づく根津権現の祭祀は廃され、当時の[[国家神道]]に基づき、本殿にはスサノオ、大山咋神、八幡神を、相殿には大国主と菅原道真を祀る形式へ強制的に改組された。 また、[[東京大学|帝国大学]]の移転にともない、江戸時代から門前に形成されていた[[根津遊廓]]は[[明治]]21年（[[1888年]]）6月末に廃され、江東区の[[洲崎 (東京都)|洲崎遊廓]]へと移転した。 == 境内 == === 社殿 === 社殿は江戸幕府第5代将軍・徳川綱吉による造営で、本殿・幣殿・拝殿が1つにまとめられた[[権現造]]の形式である。 ファイル:根津神社 本殿.JPG|本殿（重要文化財） ファイル:根津神社 拝殿.JPG|拝殿（重要文化財） ファイル:根津神社 唐門.JPG|唐門と透塀（ともに重要文化財） File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 3.jpg|透塀（重要文化財） ファイル:根津神社 神楽殿.JPG|神楽殿 ファイル:Nezu Shrine Romon 2010.jpg|楼門（重要文化財） ファイル:Komainu of Nezu Shrine left.jpg|狛犬 === 摂末社 === * 乙女稲荷神社 - 祭神：[[倉稲魂命]] : 根津神社の遷座時、境内西側の傾斜面（つつじが岡）の中腹に洞を穿つ形で祀られた。 * 駒込稲荷神社 - 祭神：[[伊弉諾命]]、[[伊弉冊命]]、[[倉稲魂命]]、[[級長津彦命]]、[[級長戸辺命]] : 寛文元年（1661年）鎮座。旧甲府藩邸時代の守り神として祀られたもので、本殿の遷座に伴って末社となった。[[浜離宮]]内の稲荷神社の分祀元。 ファイル:Otome Inari Shrine 2010.jpg|乙女稲荷神社 ファイル:駒込稲荷神社 (根津神社境内社).JPG|駒込稲荷神社 === その他 === * [[徳川家宣]][[胎盤|胞衣]]塚 - 当社遷座前、甲府藩邸時代に甲府徳川家嫡子・綱豊（のちに甲府藩主を経て6代将軍家宣となる）の胞衣（胎児を包んだ膜と[[胎盤]]）を埋めた時のもの。 * [[岐の神|塞大神]]碑 - 本郷追分（東大農学部前の[[中山道]]・[[日光御成街道]]分岐路）に祀られていた[[道祖神]]が立っている。 * 家宣の産湯井戸（非公開） * 庚申塔 - 六基。近隣の町の辻にあったものが道路拡幅などで撤去されたのを引き取ったもの。 * つつじ園 - 旧藩邸時代に家宣が植えたのが最初。現在も栽培されており、開花期（春-初夏）には一般開放されている。 * [[森鷗外]]旧邸 - 2021年（令和3年）に閉店した旅館「水月ホテル鷗外荘」（台東区池之端）にあった建物で、閉店後に移築を引き受けることになり、根津神社の総代会の全会一致で移築が決定した{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/207353|title=森鷗外が「舞姫」を執筆した旧邸、ゆかりの根津神社に移築へ 旅館として稼働もコロナ禍で閉館|date=2022-10-10|publisher=東京新聞|accessdate=2022-10-12}}。この建物は鷗外が最初の妻の赤松登志子と1889年5月から住んでいた邸宅で、1890年11月の離婚の直前の文京区への転居まで居住していた。1946年（昭和21年）に旅館の創業者が隣接する旧邸を買い取った。旅館「水月ホテル鷗外荘」での解体工事は2022年10月3日から始まり、根津神社境内東側への移設が検討されている。 File:Nezu-jinja-4.jpg|[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]4年（[[1714年]]）六代将軍[[徳川家宣]]公奉納の神輿（2010年4月24日撮影） ファイル:根津神社 胞衣塚.JPG|徳川家宣[[胎盤|胞衣]]塚（区指定文化財） ファイル:根津神社 塞大神.JPG|[[岐の神|塞大神]]碑 == 祭事 == [[ファイル:AzaleaFestivalNezuJinja.jpg|thumb|250px|right|つつじまつりの様子]] [[ファイル:Nezu jinja ōharai me no wa - c - June 30 2015.JPG|thumb|250px|right|大祓]] * 1月 ** 初詣（[[1月1日]]） * 2月 ** 節分（[[2月3日]]） * 4月-5月 ** [[文京つつじまつり|つつじまつり]] * 6月 ** 大祓 * 9月 ** 例大祭神賑行事（[[9月17日]]-[[9月18日|18日]]） ** 例祭式（[[9月21日]]） * 10月 ** 根津・千駄木下町まつり（10月20日頃） * 11月 ** 七五三詣 ** 新嘗祭（[[11月23日]]） * 12月 ** 大祓（[[12月31日]]） == 文化財 == [[File:Nezu jinja - Sukibei 2.jpg|thumb|200px|透塀（西門北方]] [[File:Nezu-jinja Außenbereich 2.jpg|thumb|200px|西門]] === 重要文化財 === ; 建造物 * 根津神社 7棟 ** 本殿・幣殿・拝殿（1棟） ** 唐門 ** 西門 ** 透塀（3棟）（唐門東方、唐門西門間、西門北方） ** 楼門 ** （附指定）銅灯籠2基 ; 工芸品 * 太刀 銘長光 * 太刀 銘備州長船秀光　康暦二年二月 日 === 文京区指定文化財 === * 神楽面 16面（彫刻） - 平成6年指定 * 神輿 3基（附 獅子2頭）（工芸） - 昭和55年指定 : 正徳4年（1714年）、遷宮記念の大祭（天下祭、宝永祭）において奉納されたもの。神輿の巡行に際して、獅子が行列を先導した。 * 徳川氏朱印状 8通（古文書） - 昭和49年指定 * 徳川家宣胞衣塚（有形民俗） - 昭和49年指定 == 氏子地域 == : [[文京区]][[根津]] : 文京区[[千駄木]]一～二丁目全域、三丁目37～51 : 文京区[[本駒込]]一丁目2～4･9～25、二丁目1～9･13～17（このうち旧駒込曙町の部分）、三丁目1～9（このうち旧駒込浅嘉町の部分） : 文京区[[向丘 (文京区)|向丘]] : 文京区[[弥生 (文京区)|弥生]] : 文京区[[本郷 (文京区)|本郷]]五丁目30～32（このうち旧本郷森川町の部分）、六丁目全域 : 文京区[[西片]]二丁目23～25 : [[台東区]][[池之端]]一丁目2～6・二丁目全域 == 現地情報 == ; 所在地 * 東京都文京区根津一丁目28番9号 ; 交通アクセス * [[ファイル:Logo of Tokyo Metro Chiyoda Line.svg|18px|C]] [[東京メトロ千代田線]] [[根津駅]]・[[千駄木駅]]（各駅とも徒歩5分） * [[ファイル:Logo of Tokyo Metro Namboku Line.svg|18px|N]] [[東京メトロ南北線]] [[東大前駅]]（徒歩5分） * [[ファイル:Toei Mita line symbol.svg|18px|I]] [[都営地下鉄三田線|都営三田線]] [[白山駅 (東京都)|白山駅]]（徒歩10分） ; 周辺 * [[日本医科大学]] * 団子坂上 - 現在の文京区立本郷図書館や東洋大学のある辺りが当社の旧鎮座地で、「元根津」とも呼ばれる。 == 登場作品 == * [[森鷗外]]『青年』 {{quotation|小泉純一は芝日蔭町の宿屋を出て、東京方眼図を片手に人にうるさく問うて、新橋停留場から上野行の電車に乗った。目まぐろしい須田町の乗換も無事に済んだ。さて本郷三丁目で電車を降りて、追分（おいわけ）から高等学校に附いて右に曲がって、根津権現の表坂上にある袖浦館という下宿屋の前に到着したのは、十月二十何日かの午前八時であった。}} * [[森鷗外]]『細木香以』[https://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000129/files/4459_18093.html　細木香以]青空文庫 {{quotation|団子坂上から南して根津権現の裏門に出る岨道に似た小径がある。これを藪下の道と云う。}} * [[高村光雲]]『幕末維新懐古談』 {{quotation|従来神田明神とか、根津権現とかいったものは、神田神社、根津神社というようになり、三社権現も浅草神社と改称して、神仏何方（どっち）かに方附けなければならないことになったのである。}} * [[岡本綺堂]]『半七捕物帳（柳原堤の女）』 {{quotation|かれは強情にかんがえた末に、同町内の和泉という建具屋の若い職人を誘い出すことにした。職人は茂八といって、ことしの夏は根津神社の境内まで素人相撲をとりに行った男である。かれは喜平の相談をうけて、一も二もなく承知した。}} * [[夏目漱石]]『道草』 {{quotation|その人は根津権現の裏門の坂を上って、彼と反対に北へ向いて歩いて来たものと見えて、健三が行手を何気なく眺めた時、十間位先から既に彼の視線に入ったのである。そうして思わず彼の眼をわきへ外させたのである。}} {{quotation|こうした無事の日が五日続いた後、六日目の朝になって帽子を被らない男は突然また根津権現の坂の蔭から現われて健三を脅やかした。それがこの前とほぼ同じ場所で、時間も殆どこの前と違わなかった。}} * [[尾崎放哉]]『入庵雑記』 {{quotation|句会は大抵根津権現さんの境内に小さい池に沿うて一寸した貸席がありましたので、其処で開きました。}} * [[岡本綺堂]]『深見夫人の死』 - 下記引用以外にも多数の記述あり {{quotation|その住宅は本郷の根津権現に近いところに在って、門を掩（おお）うている桜の大樹が昔ながらに白く咲き乱れているのも嬉しかった。}} {{quotation|わたしはその賑わいを後ろにして池（いけ）の端（はた）から根津の方角へ急いだ。その頃はまだ動坂（どうざか）行きの電車が開通していなかったので、根津の通りも暗い寂しい町であった。}} * [[寺田寅彦]]『柿の種』 {{quotation|根津権現の境内のある 旗亭 で大学生が数人会していた。 夜がふけて、あたりが静かになったころに、どこかでふくろうの鳴くのが聞こえた。 「ふくろうが鳴くね」 と一人が言った。}} * 佐々木味津三『旗本退屈男（第三話）後の旗本退屈男』 {{quotation|しかも、その六本の白刄を、笑止千万にも必死に擬していたものは、ほんの小半時前、根津権現裏のあの浪宅から、いずれともなく逐電した筈の市毛甚之丞以下おろかしき浪人共でしたから、門を堅く閉じ締めていた理由も、うしろに十数本の槍先を擬しているものの待ち伏せていた理由も、彼等六人の急を知らせたためからであったかと知った退屈男は、急にカンラカンラ打ち笑い出すと、門の外に佇んだままでいる京弥に大きく呼びかけました。}} * [[宮本百合子]]『田端の汽車そのほか』 {{quotation|森鴎外が住んでいた家は、団子坂をのぼってすぐのところにあった。坂をのぼり切ると一本はそのまま真直に肴町へ、右は林町へ折れ、左の一本は細くくねって昔太田ケ原と呼ばれた崖沿いに根津権現に出る。}} * [[林不忘]]『丹下左膳（乾雲坤竜の巻）』 {{quotation|江戸は根津権現の裏、俗に曙の里といわれるところに、神変夢想流の町道場を開いている小野塚鉄斎（おのづかてっさい）、いま奥の書院に端坐して、抜き放った一刀の刀身にあかず見入っている。}} * [[三遊亭圓朝]]（鈴木行三校訂・編纂）『敵討札所の霊験』 {{quotation|ちょうど根津権現へ参詣して、惣門内を抜けて参りましたが、只今でも全盛でございますが、昔から彼の廓は度々たびたび潰れましては又再願をして又立ったと申しますが、其の頃贅沢な女郎がございまして}} * [[夢野久作]]『東京人の堕落時代』 {{quotation|その次は井の頭で、これはどちらかと云えば高級なのが多いらしい。但、夜は高級か低級か保証の限りでない。根津権現はその又次という順序である。その他大小の公園、神社、仏閣、活動館、芝居小屋、カフェー、飲食店なぞが、色魔式の活躍場所である事は云う迄もない。}} * [[武田泰淳]]『目まいのする散歩』 {{quotation|肴町から電車通を横断して、左手に大観音（同じ宗派のその寺には、よく父親の命令でお使いに行った）を見て、根津権現の坂道にかかる。下宿屋の多い急な坂道を下りきって、上野山へと裏側から上って行く。そのあたりには、画学生の匂いがあり、やがて、美術学校や、美術館、博物館の建つ、上野山の西洋風のひろがりの中へ入る。}} == ドラマ == テレビ朝日で放送されている[[相棒]]season18第14話「善悪の彼岸～ピエタ」にて出てくる千駄木神社は、根津神社で撮影された。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist|2|refs= {{Cite == Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}} || {{ill|Jūichimen Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} || {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩}} || {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}}, with {{ill|Jūichimen Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (with {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡宮}} (with {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|ja|太田道灌}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡神}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|ja|太田氏}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|ja|徳川綱吉}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓}} in Kōtō Ward.

{{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen}} ([[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]])

As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓}} in Kōtō Ward.

{{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen}} ([[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]])
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} || {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩}} || {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (with {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡宮}} (with {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|ja|太田道灌}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.

The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡神}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|ja|太田氏}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."

As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). == Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} || {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩}} || {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (with {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡宮}} (with {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|ja|太田道灌}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡神}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|ja|太田氏}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|ja|徳川綱吉}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓}} in Kōtō Ward.
[[Category:qq]][[sn:Nezu Gongen]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nezu Gongen]][[Category:qq]]

During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|ja|太田氏}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|ja|徳川綱吉}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓}} in Kōtō Ward.

revision.text:
{{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen}} ([[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) is a god of [[Nezu Shrine]]
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} || {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩}} || {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (with {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡宮}} (with {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|ja|太田道灌}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡神}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|ja|太田氏}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|ja|徳川綱吉}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓}} in Kōtō Ward.

As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓}} in Kōtō Ward.

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen}} ([[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) is a god of [[Nezu Shrine]]
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} || {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩}} || {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (with {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡宮}} (with {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|ja|太田道灌}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡神}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|ja|太田氏}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|ja|徳川綱吉}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓}} in Kōtō Ward.
{{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Gongen]] }} [[sn:Nezu Gongen]]

Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学|12=draft}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=draft}} in Kōtō Ward.

revision.text: #redirect[[:en:根津神社#歴史]] [[Category:automatic wikipedia redirects 根]]

{{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen|12=draft}} ([[:ja:根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) is a god of [[Nezu Shrine]]
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神|12=draft}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命|12=draft}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現|12=draft}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=draft}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音|12=draft}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=draft}} || {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|12=draft}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩|12=draft}} || {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=draft}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音|12=draft}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=draft}} (with {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|12=draft}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡宮|12=draft}} (with {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|ja|太田道灌|12=draft}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=draft}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=draft}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗|12=draft}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道|12=draft}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=draft}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=draft}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡神|12=draft}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|ja|太田氏|12=draft}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=draft}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|ja|徳川綱吉|12=draft}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=draft}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重|12=draft}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城|12=draft}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継|12=draft}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=draft}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿|12=draft}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗|12=draft}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革|12=draft}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学|12=draft}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=draft}} in Kōtō Ward.
{{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]]
[[Category:Gongen]] }}
[[sn:Nezu Gongen]]

The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|ja|太田氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta clan|qq=}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|qq=}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|ja|徳川綱吉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|qq=}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|qq=}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunashige|qq=}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Castle|qq=}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ietsugu|qq=}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|qq=}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikoshi|qq=}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Yoshimune|qq=}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō Reforms|qq=}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Imperial University|qq=}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|qq=}} in Kōtō Ward.

revision.text: #redirect[[:en:根津神社#歴史]]

{{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Gongen|qq=}} ([[:ja:根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) is a god of [[Nezu Shrine]]
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Sansho Gongen|qq=}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}} || {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi Nyorai|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} || {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amida Nyorai|qq=}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|ja|十一面観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}} (with {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi Nyorai|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} (with {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amida Nyorai|qq=}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|ja|太田道灌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Dōkan|qq=}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|qq=}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|qq=}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tendai Buddhism|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai Buddhism|qq=}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Shintō|qq=}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|ja|太田氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta clan|qq=}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|qq=}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|ja|徳川綱吉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|qq=}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|qq=}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|ja|徳川綱重|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunashige|qq=}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|ja|江戸城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Castle|qq=}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ietsugu|qq=}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|qq=}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikoshi|qq=}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Yoshimune|qq=}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō Reforms|qq=}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.

Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|ja|徳川家継|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ietsugu|qq=}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|qq=}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|神輿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikoshi|qq=}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|ja|徳川吉宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Yoshimune|qq=}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|ja|享保の改革|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō Reforms|qq=}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Tokyo Imperial University|ja|帝国大学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Imperial University|qq=}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|qq=}} in Kōtō Ward.

revision.text:
{{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Gongen|qq=}} ([[:ja:根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) is a god of [[Nezu Shrine]]
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} (すさのおのみこと) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamatsumi]] (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Sansho Gongen|qq=}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} || [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Headed Kannon]] (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}} || [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} || [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, with [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Headed Kannon]] (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}} (with [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]], the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Hachiman]] (with [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]], Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by [[:en:Ōta Dōkan|Ōta Dōkan]], a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|qq=}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|qq=}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]].<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following [[:en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|Sannō Shintō]], Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and [[:en:Hachiman|Hachiman]] (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the [[:en:Ōta clan|Ōta clan]], and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|qq=}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of [[:en:Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|qq=}} (the son of his elder brother, [[:en:Tokugawa Tsunashige|Tokugawa Tsunashige]]), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsugu|Tokugawa Ietsugu]] became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|qq=}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]] becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the [[:en:Kyōhō Reforms|Kyōhō Reforms]], Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Tokyo Imperial University]] (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|qq=}} in Kōtō Ward.

These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by [[:en:Ōta Dōkan|Ōta Dōkan]], a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|qq=}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]].<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following [[:en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|Sannō Shintō]], Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and [[:en:Hachiman|Hachiman]] (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the [[:en:Ōta clan|Ōta clan]], and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of [[:en:Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣}} (the son of his elder brother, [[:en:Tokugawa Tsunashige|Tokugawa Tsunashige]]), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsugu|Tokugawa Ietsugu]] became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|qq=}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]] becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the [[:en:Kyōhō Reforms|Kyōhō Reforms]], Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Tokyo Imperial University]] (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.
