Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Headed Kannon]] (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]] (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神}} || [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Headed Kannon]] (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (with [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]], the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Hachiman]] (with [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]], Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by [[:en:Ōta Dōkan|Ōta Dōkan]], a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]].<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following [[:en:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|Sannō Shintō]], Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and [[:en:Hachiman|Hachiman]] (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the [[:en:Ōta clan|Ōta clan]], and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Bunkyō City Hongo Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of [[:en:Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, [[:en:Tokugawa Tsunashige|Tokugawa Tsunashige]]), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]], he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsugu|Tokugawa Ietsugu]] became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its [[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]] carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]] becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the [[:en:Kyōhō Reforms|Kyōhō Reforms]], Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Tokyo Imperial University]] (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.
<!-- no translated page template found in history --> [[ja:根津権現]] {{translated page|ja|根津神社#歴史|version=102499670|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神}} || {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (with {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman}} (with {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|WD=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Bunkyō City Hongo Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|WD=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|en|Tokugawa Tsunashige|WD=Q7004484|lt=Tokugawa Tsunashige}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|WD=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|en|Kyōhō Reforms|WD=Q1795223|lt=Kyōhō Reforms}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.

; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (with {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman}} (with {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|WD=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Bunkyō City Hongo Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|WD=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|en|Tokugawa Tsunashige|WD=Q7004484|lt=Tokugawa Tsunashige}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|WD=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|en|Kyōhō Reforms|WD=Q1795223|lt=Kyōhō Reforms}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] [[ja:根津神社#歴史]] {{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11536449]] Wikidata name: Nezu Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Gongen|qq=}} ([[:ja:根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) is a god of [[Nezu Shrine]]
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamatsumi}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 応神天皇|1=Ōjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317997}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神}} || {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (with {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman}} (with {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|WD=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Bunkyō City Hongo Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|WD=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|en|Tokugawa Tsunashige|WD=Q7004484|lt=Tokugawa Tsunashige}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|WD=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|en|Kyōhō Reforms|WD=Q1795223|lt=Kyōhō Reforms}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.

It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (with {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman}} (with {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|WD=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Bunkyō City Hongo Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|WD=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|en|Tokugawa Tsunashige|WD=Q7004484|lt=Tokugawa Tsunashige}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|WD=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|en|Kyōhō Reforms|WD=Q1795223|lt=Kyōhō Reforms}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] [[ja:根津神社#歴史]] {{Infobox deity}} {{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Gongen|qq=}} ([[:ja:根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) is a god of [[Nezu Shrine]]
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamatsumi}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 応神天皇|1=Ōjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317997}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神}} || {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (with {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman}} (with {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|WD=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Bunkyō City Hongo Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|WD=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|en|Tokugawa Tsunashige|WD=Q7004484|lt=Tokugawa Tsunashige}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|WD=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|en|Kyōhō Reforms|WD=Q1795223|lt=Kyōhō Reforms}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.

These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|WD=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Bunkyō City Hongo Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|WD=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|en|Tokugawa Tsunashige|WD=Q7004484|lt=Tokugawa Tsunashige}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|WD=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|en|Kyōhō Reforms|WD=Q1795223|lt=Kyōhō Reforms}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] [[ja:根津神社#歴史]] {{Infobox deity}} {{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Gongen|qq=}} ([[:ja:根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) is a god of [[Nezu Shrine]]
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 応神天皇|1=Ōjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317997}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神}} || {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (with {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman}} (with {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|WD=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Bunkyō City Hongo Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|WD=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|en|Tokugawa Tsunashige|WD=Q7004484|lt=Tokugawa Tsunashige}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|WD=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|en|Kyōhō Reforms|WD=Q1795223|lt=Kyōhō Reforms}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.

Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan|1=Ōta Dōkan}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism|1=Tiantai}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|WD=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō|1=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman|1=Hachiman}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan|1=Ōta clan}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Q17215615|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|WD=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|1=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|en|Tokugawa Tsunashige|WD=Q7004484|lt=Tokugawa Tsunashige|1=Tokugawa Tsunashige}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|WD=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu|1=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune|1=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} becoming the 8th shogun. As part of the {{ill|Kyōhō Reforms|en|Kyōhō Reforms|WD=Q1795223|lt=Kyōhō Reforms|1=Kyōhō Reforms}}, Nezu Gongen’s festival was stripped of its public funding and became a smaller, locally organized event.
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the *Shinbutsu Bunri* (神仏分離, separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the *Haibutsu Kishaku* (廃仏毀釈, anti-Buddhist movement) resulted in the abolition of the *Sannō Shintō* practices at Nezu Gongen. The shrine was forcibly reorganized according to the new {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} framework, enshrining Susanoo, Ōyamatsumi, and Hachiman in the main hall, while Ōkuninushi and Sugawara no Michizane were enshrined in the *Aiden* (相殿, secondary sanctuary).
Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University|1=Imperial Universities}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:根津権現]] [[ja:根津神社#歴史]] {{Infobox deity}} {{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Gongen|qq=}} ([[:ja:根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) is a god of [[Nezu Shrine]]
== Deities == ; Main Deities * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} (すさのおのみこと) * {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} (おおやまくいのかみ) * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 応神天皇|1=Emperor Ōjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317997}} (ほんだわけのみこと)
Under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the corresponding Shinto and Buddhist identities are as follows: {| class="wikitable" ! !! Shinto Manifestation (Former Name) !! Buddhist Identity |- | rowspan=3 | {{ill|Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja|根津三所権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nezu Sansho Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) |- | {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (Medicine Buddha) |- | {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡大菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神}} || {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} (Amitabha) |}
; Enshrined Together * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} (おおくにぬしのかみ) * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} (すがわらのみちざねこう) == History == According to the *Nezu-shi* (根津志), "The exact year of the original dedication of Nezu Daigongen is unknown. It served as the general guardian shrine of Komagome and was enshrined in Sendagi Village. The deity enshrined is {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}} (Eleven-faced Kannon) as its Buddhist counterpart. The two additional shrines enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (with {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, the Medicine Buddha, as its Buddhist counterpart) and {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman|1=Hachiman shrine}} (with {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}}, Amitabha, as its Buddhist counterpart). These three deities together were worshipped as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen* (根津三所大権現). Some say that the shrine was later revived by {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan|1=Ōta Dōkan}}, a monk known as Jishi." It is believed that about 1,900 years ago, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} founded the shrine in {{ill|Sendagi|ja|千駄木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendagi|1=Sendagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11406891}}, and during the *Bunmei* period (文明, 1469–1486), Ōta Dōkan built its shrine structures.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine was managed by {{ill|Shōzen-in|ja|昌泉院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōzen-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the sub-temple of Iōzan Shōun-ji, affiliated with {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai Buddhism|1=Tiantai}}.<ref>文京区神社誌</ref> As a *Gongen* shrine following {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|WD=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō|1=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}}, Nezu Daigongen venerated {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞烏尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as its main deity, worshipped alongside his Buddhist counterpart, Jūichimen Kannon. The shrine also enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王大権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yakushi Nyorai) and {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|WD=Q261637|lt=Hachiman|1=Hachiman}} (Amida Nyorai), collectively known as the *Nezu Sansho Gongen*.
During the *Manji* era (万治, 1658–1661), the shrine was relocated eastward due to the area being occupied by the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan|1=Ōta clan}}, and later moved again to Dangozaka (currently near {{ill|Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja|文京区立本郷図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunkyō City Hongo Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文京区立図書館|1=Q17215615|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17215615}}, former Nezu).<ref name=chimei>『東京都の地名』根津神社項。</ref> The *Edo Meishoki* (江戸名所記, published in *Kanbun* 2 / 1662) records that "Nezu Gongen is a 'Nezu' (sleepless) Gongen, perhaps serving as the sleepless guardian of all deities."
Nezu Shrine took on its current form during the reign of {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|WD=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|1=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}}, the 5th shogun of the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in *Hōei* 3 (1706). As Tsunayoshi had no heir, he designated his nephew, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja|徳川綱豊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Tsunatoyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川家宣|1=Tokugawa Ienobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q366734}} (the son of his elder brother, {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunashige|en|Tokugawa Tsunashige|WD=Q7004484|lt=Tokugawa Tsunashige|1=Tokugawa Tsunashige}}), as his successor. Since Tsunatoyo was born in the *Edo residence of the Kōfu-Tokugawa family* in Nezu, he considered Nezu Gongen his *Ubusunagami* (birthplace deity). When he moved to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}, he donated his former domain to Nezu Gongen and constructed new shrine buildings.<ref name=chimei/><ref name="nezuyuisho">[https://nedujinja.or.jp/about/ 根津神社について] - Nezu Shrine</ref> The construction was carried out by various daimyo under *Tenka-bushin* (天下普請, shogunate-funded public works). As a result, when Tsunatoyo changed his name to Tokugawa Ienobu and became the 6th shogun, and his son {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsugu|en|Tokugawa Ietsugu|WD=Q366722|lt=Tokugawa Ietsugu|1=Tokugawa Ietsugu}} became the 7th shogun, Nezu Gongen was highly revered by the {{ill|Tokugawa family|ja|徳川宗家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa family|1=Tokugawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11071640}}. Notably, during the *Shotoku* era (1714), on the *9th month, 21st day*, the shrine's grand festival (*Reisai*) was permitted to have its {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}} carried into Edo Castle, making it one of the *Tenka Matsuri* (天下祭, grand shogunate-sponsored festivals).<ref name="nezuyuisho"/> However, after the death of Ietsugu, the *Kishū-Tokugawa* lineage took over the shogunate, with {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune|1=Tokugawa Yoshimune}} becoming the 8th shogun. Additionally, with the relocation of {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University|1=Imperial Universities}} (now the University of Tokyo), the *Nezu Yūkaku* (根津遊廓, Nezu red-light district), which had flourished at the shrine's gates since the Edo period, was abolished in *Meiji* 21 (1888) and relocated to the {{ill|Susaki Yūkaku|ja|洲崎遊廓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susaki Yūkaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kōtō Ward.
